evidence: BasePlatformAdapter -> MessageEvent -> SendResult provides a clean, explicit adapter contract with normalized data types at the platform boundary
ControlAPI + HermesControl client form a well-separated REST boundary with explicit JSON shapes and stdlib-only client
Tool registry (tools/registry.py) provides a single registration seam: tools self-register at import, model_tools.py provides unified discovery/dispatch
HookRegistry fires lifecycle events with transparent sync/async support and catch-and-log error isolation

gateway/run.py contains 20 independent yaml.safe_load() calls re-parsing the same config.yaml file across _load_reasoning_config, _load_show_reasoning, _load_provider_routing, _load_fallback_model, _run_agent, session hygiene, and others -- config translation is scattered across every method boundary

_handle_message is a 700+ line method mixing authorization, command dispatch (20+ if/elif chains), session management, media enrichment, auto-transcription, session hygiene compression, agent invocation, transcript persistence, and autoreply scheduling -- orchestration does not read as composition

Temporary AIAgent instances are constructed in at least 4 places (_run_agent, _flush_memories, _handle_compress_command, session hygiene) with non-identical initialization: different max_iterations (4, 8, 90), different enabled_toolsets, different model resolution paths
_get_or_create_gateway_honcho uses hasattr(self, '_honcho_managers') despite __init__ setting these attributes, indicating boundary trust inconsistency

mid_level_elegance.dimension_character: The codebase has strong integration seams at its outer boundaries -- the platform adapter abstraction, control API, tool registry, and hook system are all clean, explicit, and well-separated. However, the central orchestration layer (GatewayRunner) is where these good boundaries converge into tangled, monolithic methods. Config reading is scattered across 20+ independent parse-and-extract sites for the same file, temporary agent construction is repeated with ad-hoc parameter variations, and the core message pipeline mixes every concern into a single 700-line method. A developer working at the platform or tool boundary would find quality strong; a developer modifying the gateway's core orchestration would encounter significant glue-code entropy and tangled back-and-forth patterns.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The outer integration seams (adapters, control API, tool registry, hooks) are genuinely well-designed and would anchor the score at 80+. But the gateway orchestration core -- where all these seams meet -- has 20 repeated config parses, a monolithic _handle_message method, and inconsistent temporary-agent construction patterns. These are not isolated rough edges; they represent systemic glue-code entropy at the most critical integration point. The score lands around 62, reflecting significant drag on a developer working at the central orchestration layer, per the 60-anchor ('seam design is tangled; a developer making a cross-module change must understand surprising implicit contracts').

issues.summary: gateway/run.py re-parses config.yaml 20+ times in independent methods instead of using a shared config object
evidence: 20 independent yaml.safe_load() calls across _load_reasoning_config, _load_show_reasoning, _load_provider_routing, _load_fallback_model, _load_background_notifications_mode, _run_agent (toolset config, progress config), _resolve_gateway_model, session hygiene, and others
Each method independently opens config.yaml, parses it, and extracts its own keys -- the config translation boundary is duplicated in every method rather than happening in one place
issues.suggestion: Create a GatewayConfig.from_yaml() or extend the existing load_gateway_config() to eagerly parse all config.yaml keys into typed fields at startup. Each method then reads from the config object instead of re-parsing YAML. For hot-reloading, add a refresh() method that re-reads once and updates all fields atomically.

issues.summary: _handle_message is a 700+ line method mixing authorization, command dispatch, media enrichment, and agent orchestration
evidence: _handle_message (line ~912) handles: user authorization and pairing, interrupt detection, command dispatch via 20+ if/elif chains, quick commands, skill commands, exec approval, session creation, context prompt building, auto-reset notices, session hygiene compression, image/audio/document enrichment, agent invocation, transcript persistence, autoreply scheduling -- all inline in one method
The orchestration does not read as composition of collaborators; each concern is woven into the linear flow with direct attribute access and inline try/except blocks
issues.suggestion: Extract the major phases into focused methods: _authorize_user(event) -> bool, _dispatch_command(event) -> Optional[str], _enrich_media(event, message_text) -> str, _persist_transcript(session_entry, ...). The top-level _handle_message becomes a ~50-line pipeline that composes these steps. This reduces the cognitive load of any single method without adding indirection layers.

issues.summary: Temporary AIAgent instances are constructed in 4+ places with inconsistent initialization parameters
evidence: Session hygiene creates AIAgent with max_iterations=4, enabled_toolsets=['memory']
_run_agent creates the real agent with max_iterations from env, full toolset, provider routing, honcho integration, callbacks, etc.
Each site independently resolves model via _resolve_gateway_model() and runtime kwargs via _resolve_runtime_agent_kwargs(), with slightly different error handling
issues.suggestion: Add a GatewayRunner._create_utility_agent(session_id, toolsets, max_iterations) helper that encapsulates the common pattern: resolve model, resolve runtime kwargs, construct AIAgent with quiet_mode=True. The 3 utility sites call this instead of duplicating construction. The main _run_agent stays separate since it has many additional concerns.

issues.summary: _get_or_create_gateway_honcho uses hasattr() to check attributes that __init__ already sets
evidence: Line 323-326: 'if not hasattr(self, "_honcho_managers"): self._honcho_managers = {}' despite __init__ (line 257) already setting self._honcho_managers = {}
Same pattern for _honcho_configs. _shutdown_gateway_honcho also uses 'getattr(self, "_honcho_managers", None)' defensively
issues.suggestion: Remove the hasattr/getattr guards since __init__ guarantees these attributes exist. The defensive checks obscure the actual contract and suggest the object might be in a partially-initialized state, which is not the case.

root_causes: GatewayRunner accumulated responsibilities incrementally (commands, media types, session hygiene, autoreply, honcho) without refactoring the central orchestration to delegate to collaborators
Config reading was added per-feature rather than being centralized, leading to scattered YAML parsing
likely_symptoms: hasattr_on_init_attributes is a symptom of the ad-hoc growth pattern -- defensive coding added during incremental feature additions

add.description: BasePlatformAdapter -> MessageEvent -> SendResult provides an explicit, minimal contract with normalized data types. All 7 platform adapters produce the same MessageEvent shape, and the gateway consumes them uniformly through set_message_handler().

ControlAPI (server) and HermesControl (client) form a clean REST boundary. The client uses only stdlib (urllib), the server has a focused 5-endpoint surface. External tools interact without coupling to gateway internals.

add.header: Tool registry as single registration seam
add.description: tools/registry.py provides a single ToolEntry registration point. Tools self-register at import time via registry.register(), and model_tools.py provides unified get_definitions/dispatch. No parallel data structures needed by consumers.

add.header: Hook system with isolated error propagation
add.description: HookRegistry.emit() fires lifecycle events with catch-and-log error isolation, transparent sync/async handler support, and wildcard matching. Hooks never block the main pipeline -- a predictable error propagation pattern at this boundary.

20+ independent yaml.safe_load() calls in gateway/run.py each open, parse, and extract from config.yaml independently. Config translation at boundaries happens in 20 different places instead of one obvious place, creating glue-code entropy.

The core message pipeline is a 700+ line method mixing authorization, command dispatch, media enrichment, session hygiene, agent invocation, and transcript persistence inline. Orchestration does not read as composition of collaborators.

Temporary AIAgent instances for compression, memory flush, and session hygiene are constructed in 4+ places with varying parameters (different max_iterations, toolsets, model resolution). The handoff pattern at the gateway->agent boundary is not consistent.
